front: How does Iceberg achieve ACID transactions on object storage?
back: Iceberg achieves ACID by using optimistic concurrency control at the metadata level. Writers create new data files and a new snapshot, then attempt to atomically swap the current metadata pointer (metadata.json) to reference the new snapshot. On S3, this atomic swap uses: (1) the Hadoop catalog uses HDFS atomic rename, (2) the Hive Metastore catalog uses a conditional update, (3) REST catalogs use Compare-And-Swap operations. If two writers race, only one wins — the loser retries with a fresh snapshot. Isolation is snapshot-based: readers always see a consistent snapshot and are never blocked by writers (MVCC semantics). Atomicity: either the entire write is committed (new snapshot) or nothing changes. Durability: all data files are written to object storage before the metadata swap. The limitation: no row-level locking — high-concurrency write workloads with many conflicting writers will experience retry overhead. Design for append-heavy or partitioned writes to minimize conflicts.
